one_line_takeaway: ImageProtector shows that visual prompt injection -- normally framed purely as an attack on MLLMs -- can be repurposed defensively: a nearly imperceptible, white-box-optimized image perturbation reliably (86-98% average refusal rate across 6 open-weight MLLMs and 4 datasets) forces any MLLM query about the image into a refusal regardless of the question asked, though the approach requires gradient access to the target model(s) and degrades under multi-round questioning or determined attacker-side countermeasures (which themselves cost the attacker accuracy or compute).
key_finding: On LLaVA-1.5/VQAv2 (Table 2), ImageProtector achieves refusal rates of 0.94/0.88/0.88 (exact/similar/general shadow questions) versus 0.65/0.62/0.51 for the adapted Bagdasaryan et al. baseline and near-zero (0.02) for the adapted Qi et al. baseline; ImageProtector+PGD matches effectiveness (0.94/0.91/0.91) but at higher compute cost (Table 3: 45.6-61.2 GPU-minutes/image for PGD vs 10.2-45.6 for BIM). Clean accuracy on unperturbed images is 0.92 but collapses to 0.03-0.04 under ImageProtector across all shadow-question types (Table 4), i.e. the defense also destroys the attacker's ability to get a usable answer, not merely triggers a refusal string. Across the full 6-MLLM x 4-dataset grid (Tables 5-7), average refusal rates are 0.95 (exact), 0.91 (similar), 0.86 (general) for image-relevant questions and slightly lower for image-irrelevant questions; InstructBLIP is consistently the hardest target (lowest refusal rates, attributed to its larger Q-Former-based projector) and CelebA (facial images) consistently yields the highest refusal rates across all MLLMs. A single universal perturbation can be jointly optimized against multiple target MLLMs simultaneously (Figure 4), e.g. 0.90/0.80/0.80 refusal rates against LLaVA-1.5/MiniGPT-4/Qwen-VL-Chat jointly. On countermeasures: Gaussian noise at sigma=0.02 drops ImageProtector's refusal rate from >0.90 to near zero but degrades clean accuracy from 0.92 to ~0.80; DiffPure at one timestep drops refusal rate from >0.90 to <0.20 (two timesteps to near zero) but drops accuracy to 0.82/0.78 respectively and adds 8.0%/13.1% inference overhead; adversarial training over three epochs still leaves ImageProtector's refusal rate around 60% while significantly degrading target-model accuracy. The paper's overall claim is that none of these three countermeasures fully negates the protection without a nontrivial accuracy or efficiency cost to the attacker's own pipeline.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is ImageProtector, a user-side, white-box, gradient-based method that embeds a nearly imperceptible ell-infinity-bounded perturbation into an image so that any open-weight multi-modal LLM (MLLM) queried about that image with ANY probing question is induced to output a generic refusal response, rather than answering. The authors formalize this as a constrained optimization problem: maximize the probability (via cross-entropy loss over a sampled refusal-response token sequence) that a set of target MLLMs, when given the perturbed image plus a set of 'shadow' probing questions, output a refusal, subject to an L_infinity norm bound on the perturbation (used as the utility/imperceptibility constraint). They solve this with the Basic Iterative Method (BIM), sign-gradient updates with L_infinity projection each iteration (Algorithm 1), and compare against a PGD variant. The explicitly claimed novelty (stated directly by the authors, not attributed to prior work) is repurposing visual prompt injection -- normally an offensive/jailbreak technique -- as a defensive, opt-in privacy mechanism: 'formalize a novel problem: preventing MLLMs from analyzing images' and 'first to demonstrate this potential, shifting prompt injection from a purely offensive technique to a tool for safeguarding user images.' They also introduce a three-tier taxonomy of 'shadow questions' (exact, similar, general) that the image owner uses in place of the attacker's actual unknown probing questions, and evaluate three candidate countermeasures (Gaussian noise, DiffPure, adversarial training) as the paper's own robustness analysis, showing each trades off refusal-suppression against accuracy/efficiency degradation on clean images.
threat_model: Defender-oriented threat model, explicitly stated in Section 3.2. The 'attacker' is a malicious actor who downloads a user's publicly posted image and uses an open-weight MLLM (chosen because open-weight models are cheap, locally runnable, and not rate-limited/monitored like commercial APIs) to extract sensitive personal information (identity, location, private details) via arbitrary probing questions; the attacker has full, unmodified access to the MLLM and the image, and the paper assumes the attacker cannot be prevented from asking whatever questions they want. The 'defender' is the image owner, who has white-box access (including gradients) to one or more target open-weight MLLMs the attacker is anticipated to use, and can only modify the image itself before publishing it -- they cannot alter MLLM parameters/training or intercept/alter the attacker's queries. The defender does not know the attacker's exact question in advance, so must generalize across a set of self-generated 'shadow questions' (exact/similar/general, generated via an LLM such as GPT-4). The paper explicitly scopes out closed-source/black-box MLLMs as a stated limitation, since ImageProtector requires gradient access.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Limitations section): (1) effectiveness degrades in multi-round VQA / longer context -- for general shadow questions, refusal rate drops from 88% to ~70% across repeated rounds of the same probing question, while exact/similar shadow questions decay less than 5%; (2) the method is image-only and does not address audio/video modalities in emerging multimodal models; (3) the core white-box assumption (gradient access to target MLLMs) is a hard limitation that excludes closed-source/API-only MLLMs -- the authors explicitly scope this out rather than attempt a black-box/surrogate-model extension, noting it would require fundamentally different (query-based or surrogate) techniques. Additional grounded caveats visible in the results themselves (not explicitly flagged by authors as limitations but present in the paper's own tables): all images are downsampled to a fixed 224x224 resolution for multi-model optimization convenience, which the authors note is a controlled simplification rather than a requirement; Phi-4-multimodal-instruct and Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct were excluded from the multi-target-MLLM experiments due to incompatible dependency/image-size requirements; effectiveness is judged by a GPT-4 refusal classifier (an LLM-as-judge dependency, whose own error rate is not separately reported in the visible text); and refusal-rate variance across MLLMs is nontrivial (e.g., InstructBLIP substantially lower than others), meaning the universal-perturbation claim is stronger for some architectures than others.

quantitative_results: Table 2 (LLaVA-1.5, VQAv2): ImageProtector refusal rate 0.94/0.88/0.88 (exact/similar/general shadow questions) vs No Perturbation 0.00/0.00/0.00, Qi et al. 0.02/0.02/0.02, Bagdasaryan et al. 0.65/0.62/0.51, ImageProtector+PGD 0.94/0.91/0.91
Table 3 (GPU-minutes per image, LLaVA-1.5/VQAv2): ImageProtector 10.2 (exact) / 45.6 (similar) / 45.6 (general) vs ImageProtector+PGD 16.2/61.2/61.2
Table 4 (LLaVA-1.5/VQAv2 accuracy): No Perturbation 0.92; Qi et al. 0.48; Bagdasaryan et al. 0.03-0.04; ImageProtector 0.03-0.04; ImageProtector+PGD 0.03-0.04
Tables 5-7 (avg refusal rate across 6 MLLMs x 4 datasets): exact-shadow image-relevant avg 0.95 / image-irrelevant avg 0.94; similar-shadow 0.91/0.90; general-shadow 0.86/0.84
Table 5: Phi-4-multimodal reaches 1.00 avg refusal rate (exact, image-relevant); InstructBLIP lowest at 0.93 (still comparatively low across tables)
CelebA consistently yields the highest per-dataset refusal rates across all 6 MLLMs and all 3 shadow-question types

Figure 4 (multi-target optimization, VQAv2, general shadow questions): joint LLaVA-1.5+MiniGPT-4+Qwen-VL-Chat perturbation achieves refusal rates 0.90/0.80/0.80 on the three respective evaluation models
Gaussian noise countermeasure: refusal rate drops from >0.90 (sigma=0) to near zero at sigma=0.02; clean accuracy drops from 0.92 to ~0.80 at sigma=0.02

DiffPure countermeasure: 1 timestep drops refusal rate from >0.90 to <0.20 and accuracy from 0.92 to 0.82 (+8.0% inference time); 2 timesteps drops refusal rate to near zero and accuracy to 0.78 (+13.1% inference time)
Adversarial training countermeasure (LLaVA-1.5/VQAv2, 3 epochs): ImageProtector refusal rate remains ~60% while target-model accuracy 'significantly decreases'
Limitations section: multi-round degradation -- general shadow questions refusal rate falls from 88% to ~70% across repeated rounds; exact/similar shadow questions decay <5%

Ablation (Appendix C, summarized in body): optimal step size alpha ~0.007 for exact shadow questions vs ~0.005 for similar/general; performance on similar questions degrades after 1500 iterations (overfitting); epsilon=8/255 is the most effective perturbation bound; generalization stabilizes with mini-batch size >=3 and >=40 shadow questions
baselines_compared: Qi et al. 2024 (visual adversarial jailbreak attack, adapted with empty shadow-question set)
Bagdasaryan et al. 2023 (token-by-token visual prompt injection attack, adapted for refusal-inducing objective)
ImageProtector+PGD (ablation: projected gradient descent instead of BIM as the optimizer)
